question:
River courier Seo is a veteran who has worked on this river for 9 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 114km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 7 units of construction material (53kg each) and 4 units of medical kit (6kg each). The boat can travel at 45km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 3km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 25km is Zone A (speed limit: 29km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 24km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1700kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 12% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 15%.
From 9:00 AM to 10:00 AM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 18%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 45km waypoint, 4 units of construction material are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 42 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 37 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 6 minutes (1st: 37min, 2nd: 43min, 3rd: 49min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Riverside logistics driver Yoon is a veteran who has worked on this river for 6 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 101km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 8 units of medical kit (15kg each) and 3 units of water barrel (18kg each). The boat can travel at 45km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 2km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 15km is Zone A (speed limit: 37km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 23km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1700kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 18%.
From 12:00 PM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 14%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 81km waypoint, 1 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 42 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 40 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 7 minutes (1st: 40min, 2nd: 47min, 3rd: 54min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

In Zone B, current aids (downstream) so effective speed = boat speed + current speed. However speed limits apply to effective speed.

Boat speed in still water is 45 km/h. Current = 2 km/h.

Thus theoretical effective speeds: In Zone A: 45 - 2 = 43 km/h (max possible). Zone B: 45 + 2 = 47 km/h.

But speed limits: Zone A speed limit 37 km/h (effective). Zone B speed limit 23 km/h (effective). So boat must limit its speed to effective speed ≤ limit.

So cargo does NOT exceed threshold. Therefore no reduction of speed limits. So initial speed limits remain 37 and 23 km/h.

Congestion period 12:00-14:00: speed limits reduced further by 14% (applied after cargo regulation - none). So during that interval, effective speed limit becomes 86% of original.

The first 23km is Zone A (speed limit: 25km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 23km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (2400kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 11% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 18%.

At the 45km waypoint, 9 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 45 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 36 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 7 minutes (1st: 36min, 2nd: 43min, 3rd: 50min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Cargo transporter Park is a veteran who has worked on this river for 14 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 104km. He departs at 9:00 AM carrying 8 units of relief supply box (37kg each) and 6 units of water barrel (18kg each). The boat can travel at 49km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 3km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 22km is Zone A (speed limit: 37km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 23km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (2100kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 15%.
From 11:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 18%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 63km waypoint, 2 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 43 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 39 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 8 minutes (1st: 39min, 2nd: 47min, 3rd: 55min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

We need to compute travel time given many constraints.

Given: total distance upstream = 104 km. Starting at 9:00 AM.

Boat speed in still water = 49 km/h. River current = 3 km/h.

Zone A is upstream (current opposes), effective speed = boat speed - current = 49 - 3 = 46 km/h (in still water, but there are speed limits). Zone B downstream: effective speed = boat speed + current = 49 + 3 = 52 km/h.

But speed limits apply to effective speed after accounting for current. So we have initial speed limits: Zone A limit = 37 km/h; Zone B limit = 23 km/h. However, actual effective speed may be limited to those values (i.e., boat cannot exceed that effective speed). Also cargo weight may reduce speed limits: if cargo weight > 2100 kg, then Zone A limit reduced by 16%; Zone B limit reduced by 15%.

We need to calculate cargo weight at start: 8 units relief box each 37 kg = 8*37 = 296 kg. 6 water barrels each 18 kg = 108 kg. Total = 404 kg. That's far below 2100 kg, so cargo threshold not exceeded. Wait maybe we need to consider box weight incorrectly? 8*37=296, 6*18=108 -> 404 indeed. So not exceed, so no reduction.

But later after unloading 2 water barrels at 63km waypoint, cargo weight reduces further, still below threshold.

Thus cargo regulation doesn't affect speed limits (since weight below threshold). So speed limits remain 37 km/h in Zone A, 23 km/h in Zone B, before congestion adjustments.

Now congestion period 11:00 to 13:00: speed limits in all zones reduced by 18% (applied on top of cargo regulation). So during that interval, the permissible effective speed limit is (original limit)*(1-0.18) = 0.82 * limit.

Thus during congestion, Zone A limit = 37*0.82 = 30.34 km/h; Zone B limit = 23*0.82 = 18.86 km/h.

But also must respect that effective speed due to current cannot exceed these limits. The boat's natural effective speeds (46 km/h upstream, 52 km/h downstream) exceed limits, so basically the limits are binding. So boat will travel at the limited effective speed (subject to congestion factor if time falls in that window). So we have piecewise speed.

Now rest regulations: boat cannot operate continuously >43 minutes (i.e., must rest after at most 43 minutes). Rest allowed only at designated rest points every 9 km. So rest points at distances: 0 km (perhaps base), 9 km, 18 km, 27 km, 36 km, 45 km, 54 km, 63 km, 72 km, 81 km, 90 km, 99 km, and maybe also final 104 (but not rest point). Rest points also coincide with the 63km where unloading occurs (which is also a rest point). That's convenient.

The river has a current of 2km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 17km is Zone A (speed limit: 33km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 24km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (2500kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 11% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 16%.
From 1:00 PM to 3:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 14%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 90km waypoint, 2 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 44 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 33 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 7 minutes (1st: 33min, 2nd: 40min, 3rd: 47min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Inland navigator Jung is a veteran who has worked on this river for 10 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 99km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 14 units of medical kit (12kg each) and 4 units of construction material (76kg each). The boat can travel at 52km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 2km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 16km is Zone A (speed limit: 35km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 25km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1800kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 11% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 14%.
From 11:00 AM to 12:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 17%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 27km waypoint, 9 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 42 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 39 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 8 minutes (1st: 39min, 2nd: 47min, 3rd: 55min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:

He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 10 units of relief supply box (35kg each) and 5 units of water barrel (19kg each). The boat can travel at 45km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 3km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 17km is Zone A (speed limit: 31km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 23km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 14%.
From 10:00 AM to 11:00 AM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 12%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 81km waypoint, 5 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 45 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 33 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 6 minutes (1st: 33min, 2nd: 39min, 3rd: 45min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 12 units of water barrel (18kg each) and 2 units of relief supply box (31kg each). The boat can travel at 45km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 2km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 22km is Zone A (speed limit: 27km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 25km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (2300kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 15% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 17%.
From 10:00 AM to 11:00 AM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 18%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 72km waypoint, 4 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 43 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 45 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 8 minutes (1st: 45min, 2nd: 53min, 3rd: 61min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Freight captain Han is a veteran who has worked on this river for 6 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 110km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 15 units of water barrel (23kg each) and 3 units of construction material (52kg each). The boat can travel at 51km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 3km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 22km is Zone A (speed limit: 35km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 24km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (2300kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 15% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 18%.
From 10:00 AM to 11:00 AM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 11%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 45km waypoint, 10 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 44 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 29 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 7 minutes (1st: 29min, 2nd: 36min, 3rd: 43min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

The river has a current of 3km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 17km is Zone A (speed limit: 34km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 25km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (2400kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 11% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 14%.
From 11:00 AM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 12%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 36km waypoint, 3 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 42 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 42 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 8 minutes (1st: 42min, 2nd: 50min, 3rd: 58min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Riverside logistics driver Yoon is a veteran who has worked on this river for 12 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 106km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 7 units of water barrel (23kg each) and 6 units of construction material (50kg each). The boat can travel at 38km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 3km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 18km is Zone A (speed limit: 25km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 23km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1800kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 15% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 16%.
From 12:00 PM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 13%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 63km waypoint, 2 units of water barrel are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 44 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 44 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 7 minutes (1st: 44min, 2nd: 51min, 3rd: 58min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 45 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 26 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 6 minutes (1st: 26min, 2nd: 32min, 3rd: 38min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Freight captain Han is a veteran who has worked on this river for 9 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 106km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 6 units of medical kit (8kg each) and 3 units of relief supply box (47kg each). The boat can travel at 49km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 3km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 18km is Zone A (speed limit: 38km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 24km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1500kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 12% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 18%.
From 11:00 AM to 12:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 16%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 54km waypoint, 1 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 43 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 25 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 7 minutes (1st: 25min, 2nd: 32min, 3rd: 39min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

Also speed limits apply to effective speed (boat speed ± current). Boat speed in still water = 49 km/h. Current = 3 km/h. In Zone A (upstream), effective speed = 49 - 3 = 46 km/h before speed limit. In Zone B (downstream), effective speed = 49 + 3 = 52 km/h before speed limit.

But each zone has speed limit (max effective speed). Zone A limit 38 km/h (after adjustments). Zone B limit 24 km/h. So actual effective speed will be min(46, limit) after reductions. Let's compute.

First cargo weight: 6 units medical kit 8kg each = 48 kg. 3 units relief box 47 kg each = 141 kg. Total = 189 kg. Way below 1500kg. So cargo does NOT exceed threshold, so no reduction initially. Good.

Thus speed limits are as given: Zone A 38 km/h, Zone B 24 km/h.

Now congestion period 11:00-12:00 reduces speed limits by additional 16% (applied on top of cargo regulation). So effective speed limits become 38 * (1 - 0.16) = 31.92 km/h in Zone A during that hour, and 24 * 0.84 = 20.16 km/h in Zone B.

Now we need to compute timeline. Start at 8:00 AM. Travel 106 km total. Zones: first 18 km Zone A, remaining 88 km Zone B? Wait total 106km, first 18 km Zone A, then Zone B covers 106-18 = 88 km. Good.

But also there is unload at 54 km waypoint. That is measured from start. So 54 km is within Zone B (since beyond 18). At 54 km we unload 1 relief box (47 kg). After unloading, cargo weight reduces to 189-47 = 142 kg, still below threshold.

Thus cargo regulation never triggers reduction.

Thus speed limits only affected by congestion hour.

Now need consider rest points every 9 km. They are at distances: 9,18,27,36,45,54,63,72,81,90,99,108 (but final 106, so last before destination is 99). Rest allowed only at those points. question:
Freight captain Han is a veteran who has worked on this river for 8 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 111km. He departs at 8:00 AM carrying 13 units of relief supply box (60kg each) and 2 units of medical kit (11kg each). The boat can travel at 46km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 3km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 21km is Zone A (speed limit: 34km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 23km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (2400kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 14%.
From 12:00 PM to 2:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 11%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 45km waypoint, 7 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 43 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 45 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 7 minutes (1st: 45min, 2nd: 52min, 3rd: 59min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

question:
Ferryman Kim is a veteran who has worked on this river for 13 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 106km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 7 units of relief supply box (56kg each) and 6 units of water barrel (18kg each). The boat can travel at 44km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 2km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 15km is Zone A (speed limit: 32km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 25km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (2400kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 14% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 11%.
From 9:00 AM to 10:00 AM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 11%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 45km waypoint, 4 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 43 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 25 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 8 minutes (1st: 25min, 2nd: 33min, 3rd: 41min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

Safety weight threshold 2400 kg, far higher than cargo weight (500 kg). So cargo does NOT exceed threshold. So speed limits are not reduced by cargo. So base limits: Zone A limit 32 km/h, Zone B limit 25 km/h.

Congestion 9-10 AM reduces speed limits further by 11% (applied after cargo regulation, but cargo regulation didn't change). So during that hour, limits become: Zone A limit *0.89 = 32 *0.89 = 28.48 km/h. Zone B limit: 25*0.89 = 22.25 km/h.

The first 23km is Zone A (speed limit: 33km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 23km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1500kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 16% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 14%.
From 12:00 PM to 1:00 PM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 17%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 45km waypoint, 2 units of relief supply box are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 42 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 25 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 8 minutes (1st: 25min, 2nd: 33min, 3rd: 41min, ...).

question:
Riverside logistics driver Yoon is a veteran who has worked on this river for 6 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 99km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 6 units of water barrel (24kg each) and 4 units of medical kit (13kg each). The boat can travel at 44km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 2km/h. In Zone B, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone A, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 15km is Zone A (speed limit: 36km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 24km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1700kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 10% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 13%.
From 9:00 AM to 11:00 AM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 10%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 27km waypoint, 2 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 44 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 47 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 6 minutes (1st: 47min, 2nd: 53min, 3rd: 59min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)

Zone B effective = 44 + 2 = 46 km/h before speed limit.

But there are speed limits: Zone A limit 36 km/h effective speed; Zone B limit 24 km/h effective speed.

Now cargo weight: 6 water barrels each 24 kg = 144 kg. 4 medical kits each 13 kg = 52 kg. Total initial cargo = 196 kg. Threshold is 1700 kg, far above, so cargo not exceeding threshold. So cargo regulation never triggers. Good.

Congestion period 9:00-11:00 reduces speed limits in all zones by additional 10% applied on top of any cargo reduction. So speed limits become 0.9 * original limit (since no cargo reduction). So Zone A limit = 36 *0.9 = 32.4 km/h effective. Zone B limit = 24 *0.9 = 21.6 km/h effective while within congestion period.

Outside congestion, speed limits revert to original (36 and 24) because no cargo reduction.

question:
Marine operator Choi is a veteran who has worked on this river for 12 years and has been assigned to transport goods to an upstream region spanning a total of 103km. He departs at 7:00 AM carrying 13 units of water barrel (15kg each) and 3 units of medical kit (8kg each). The boat can travel at 46km/h in still water.
The river has a current of 3km/h. In Zone A, the current is downstream (effective speed = boat speed + current speed), while in Zone B, the current is upstream (effective speed = boat speed - current speed).
The first 18km is Zone A (speed limit: 38km/h), followed by Zone B (speed limit: 24km/h). Speed limits apply to the effective speed after accounting for the current.
If the cargo exceeds the safety weight threshold (1600kg), the Zone A speed limit is reduced by 10% and the Zone B speed limit is reduced by 14%.
From 10:00 AM to 11:00 AM is a congestion period, during which the speed limits in all zones are additionally reduced by 14%. This reduction is applied on top of the speed limit after the cargo regulation has been applied.
At the 36km waypoint, 1 units of medical kit are to be unloaded. After unloading, the cargo regulation is reapplied based on the remaining cargo weight.
The boat cannot operate continuously for more than 44 minutes. Rest is only permitted at designated rest points (every 9km). The base rest duration is 31 minutes. However, due to cumulative fatigue regulations, each rest period increases by 7 minutes (1st: 31min, 2nd: 38min, 3rd: 45min, ...).
Following all of the above rules, what is the total travel time including mandatory rest stops to reach the final destination? First calculate the total travel time as an integer number of minutes, then convert to hours and minutes. (e.g., 1450 minutes total → 24 hours 10 minutes)
